Title: Plant cuticles repress organ initiation and development during skotomorphogenesis in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes SAM activation, organ initiation, and cell division
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Shoot apical meristem and developing aerial tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: IPT5 expression is increased in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4, supporting elevated cytokinin biosynthesis associated with premature true leaf initiation in darkness
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; pharmacological inhibition of cytokinin biosynthesis with lovastatin
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IPT5 expression is upregulated in cuticle mutants. 2. Increased cytokinin biosynthesis contributes to SAM activation and leaf growth in darkness. 3. Inhibiting cytokinin biosynthesis suppresses the mutant leaf-initiation phenotype.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in dark-grown bdg1-4 and lacs2-4 than WT
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Elevated in mutant SAM-associated tissues during darkness
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark growth; lovastatin treatment; hypoxia treatment
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated cytokinin may disturb normal skotomorphogenesis and developmental timing
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential lever to tune meristem activity, but strong activation can cause inappropriate organ initiation under dark or stress conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a core cutin gene but directly tied to the validated cytokinin mechanism downstream of cuticle defects.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes SAM activation and cell division
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Shoot apical meristem and developing aerial tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: IPT7 is induced in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4 and contributes with IPT genes to increased cytokinin activity underlying premature leaf initiation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; lovastatin treatment; genetic interaction with ipt3/5/7
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IPT7 expression is elevated in cuticle mutants. 2. The cytokinin biosynthetic module including IPT genes is required for the premature leaf-initiation phenotype. 3. ipt3/5/7 suppresses bdg1-4 SAM activation in darkness.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in mutants than WT under darkness
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess cytokinin can disturb normal etiolated growth
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for developmental timing manipulation, with caution for pleiotropy
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of a validated downstream cytokinin mechanism.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CYCD3;3 is activated in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4, and cycd3;3 mutation greatly rescues premature leaf initiation and growth in the cuticle mutants
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; CYCB1;1::GUS cell-division reporter; genetic crosses with cycd3;3
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cell-cycle genes including CYCD3;3 are induced in cuticle mutants. 2. Active cell division occurs in SAM and RAM of bdg1-4 and lacs2-4. 3. cycd3;3 partially suppresses mutant true-leaf growth in darkness, showing that derepressed cell-cycle progression is causal.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Elevated in dark-grown bdg1-4 and lacs2-4
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in mutant meristems during darkness
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced cell division may cause premature organogenesis and altered growth allocation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A candidate for tuning meristem growth, but likely high pleiotropic risk
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because direct genetic suppression demonstrated a core causal role downstream of cuticle defects.

Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq; raw data deposited at ScienceDB
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AMP-binding acyl-activating enzyme family; long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cutin monomer activation for cutin polyester assembly
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Long-chain fatty acids, especially C16/C18 hydroxy or epoxy fatty acids for cutin monomer activation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates long-chain fatty acids by converting them to acyl-CoA thioesters for cutin monomer synthesis and polyester formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Forms the aerial cuticle that protects against desiccation and environmental stresses and, in this study, maintains proper skotomorphogenesis and SAM quiescence
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Aerial epidermal cuticle, including cotyledons, apical hook, and SAM epidermis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies activated precursors required for cutin polymer formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key cutin biosynthesis gene; bottleneck not explicitly established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of LACS2 impairs cutin biosynthesis and cuticle integrity, causing short hypocotyls, opened apical hook/cotyledons, and ectopic SAM activation with true leaf growth in darkness
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS mutant mapping/BSA-seq; allelism test with lacs2-760; cuticle permeability assay; TEM of SAM cuticle; cutin monomer profiling; RNA-seq; RT-qPCR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. lacs2-4 was mapped to a conserved G417D lesion in LACS2 and behaves as a loss-of-function allele. 2. LACS2 is required for proper cutin biosynthesis, especially normal accumulation of C18:1, C18:2, and C18:3 dicarboxylic acids in etiolated seedlings. 3. Loss of LACS2 disrupts cuticle structure and triggers de-etiolated development in darkness, including hook opening and SAM activation. 4. LACS2 expression increases after germination in darkness but is rapidly repressed by light signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression gradually increases after germination in darkness; rapidly repressed by light; lower in PHYBY276H, PHYAY242H, and cop1-4 in darkness; higher in phyA-201 and phyB-5 in light
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High during dark-grown seedling development in cotyledons/SAM including apical hook; strongly reduced upon light exposure
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark growth; transfer to light; photoreceptor and cop1 mutant backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cuticle defects associated with abnormal development, impaired hook maintenance, reduced hypocotyl elongation, altered hormone responses, and premature SAM activation
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native promoter; no transgene promoter used for LACS2 functional validation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating LACS2 could tune cuticle formation and developmental transitions, but loss of function causes severe cuticle and growth defects, so engineering should favor conditional/tissue-specific modulation rather than knockout
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study focuses on developmental regulation rather than nutritional quality traits; metabolite output is cutin and specific C18 dicarboxylic acid cutin monomers.

Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq; raw data deposited at ScienceDB
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cutin polyester formation, especially unsaturated C18 cutin monomer branch
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Cutin monomer intermediates, particularly unsaturated C18-derived components
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Properly assembled cutin polyester enriched in C18 unsaturated-derived monomers
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions in cutin polyester biosynthesis/assembly required for formation of a continuous cuticle, particularly affecting unsaturated C18 cutin monomer incorporation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Forms the aerial cuticle that protects against desiccation and environmental stresses and maintains dark-grown developmental repression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Aerial epidermal cuticle, including SAM epidermis, cotyledons, and apical hook
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/assembly of the target cutin polymer
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key cutin biosynthesis gene; bottleneck not explicitly established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of BDG1 impairs cuticle structure and reduces unsaturated C18 cutin monomers, leading to de-etiolated growth and SAM activation in darkness
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS mutant mapping/BSA-seq; allelism test with bdg1-202; toluidine blue staining; TEM; cutin monomer profiling; RNA-seq; RT-qPCR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bdg1-4 was mapped to a conserved V315M lesion in BDG1 and behaves as a loss-of-function allele. 2. BDG1 is essential for proper cuticle integrity and mainly affects synthesis/accumulation of C18:1, C18:2, and C18:3 dicarboxylic acid cutin monomers. 3. bdg1 mutants show opened hook/cotyledons, short hypocotyl, and activated SAM in darkness. 4. BDG1 expression rises during dark growth and is rapidly repressed by light signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression gradually increases after germination in darkness; rapidly repressed by light; reduced in constitutive light-signaling backgrounds in darkness
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in dark-grown cotyledons/SAM including apical hook; quickly repressed after transfer to light
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark growth; light transfer; phyA/phyB/cop1 signaling backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes compromised cuticle, altered oxygen and hormone homeostasis, reduced hypocotyl elongation, and premature organogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native promoter; no transgene promoter used for BDG1 functional validation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: BDG1 is a leverage point for cuticle engineering, but constitutive reduction is deleterious; deployment would require careful temporal or tissue-specific regulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper links BDG1-mediated cutin biosynthesis to developmental repression rather than food nutrient quality; nonetheless it is a clear metabolism gene controlling lipid polyester accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RT-qPCR expression analysis; prior phenotype comparison; raw RNA-seq deposited at ScienceDB
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cutin monomer acyltransferase step in cutin polyester formation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Cutin monomer esters contributing to cutin polymer formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers acyl groups during cutin monomer synthesis required for cutin polyester assembly
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Protective cuticle component required here for repression of organ initiation during skotomorphogenesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly contributes to cutin monomer formation for polymer assembly
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key pathway gene but not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: dcr-4 mutants phenocopy bdg1 and lacs2 with short hypocotyl, opened apical hook/cotyledons, and active SAM in darkness
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant phenotyping; RT-qPCR expression under dark/light conditions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DCR knockout seedlings show de-etiolated growth in darkness similar to bdg1 and lacs2 mutants. 2. DCR is grouped with BDG1 and LACS2 as a key cutin biosynthesis gene required for proper skotomorphogenesis. 3. DCR expression increases in darkness after germination and is rapidly repressed by light.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Gradually induced after germination in darkness and rapidly repressed by light; reduced in PHYBY276H, PHYAY242H, and cop1-4 in darkness
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in dark-grown cotyledons/SAM including apical hook; low after light treatment
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cuticle deficiency-associated developmental defects under dark growth
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful marker/target for cuticle engineering, but knockout is deleterious to normal development
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core pathway comparator because its mutant phenotype was directly tested in Results.

Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; GUS hypoxia reporter assays; raw data deposited at ScienceDB
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Hypoxia-responsive developmental control linked to SAM organ initiation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Represses class III HD-ZIP protein activity; promotes organ initiation under hypoxia
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Antagonizes/represses class III HD-ZIP activity, accompanied by reduced PHB/PHV/REV expression in cuticle mutants
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of SAM activation and true leaf initiation in dark-grown cuticle mutants
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Induced ZPR2 contributes to premature organ initiation and true leaf growth in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4 during skotomorphogenesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; genetic crosses with zpr2 mutant rescuing bdg1-4 and lacs2-4 leaf-initiation phenotypes; hypoxia reporter analyses
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZPR2 is specifically induced in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4, whereas ZPR1/3/4 are not. 2. Elevated ZPR2 correlates with reduced PHB/PHV/REV expression and lower oxygen status in mutants. 3. Mutation of ZPR2 largely rescues the premature leaf initiation phenotype of bdg1-4 and lacs2-4. 4. Hypoxia induces ZPR2 expression and links cuticle defects to SAM activation.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly induced in dark-grown bdg1-4 and lacs2-4; induced by hypoxia
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in mutant SAM-associated tissues under darkness and in hypoxia
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark growth; hypoxia (5% O2); lovastatin treatment examined
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts through the ZPR2-class III HD-ZIP regulatory module
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated ZPR2 derepresses organogenesis in darkness, potentially disrupting developmental timing
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate meristem activation timing, but manipulation may broadly alter organogenesis and polarity networks
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a metabolic enzyme; included because it is a core experimentally validated regulator connecting cuticle status to developmental and oxygen-response outputs.

Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; HLS1::GUS; genetic rescue by 35S::HLS1
Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Developmental regulator in ethylene/auxin-responsive hook formation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Auxin-responsive developmental program during apical hook formation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive regulation of apical hook development downstream of cuticle-dependent signaling
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Cuticle status; auxin; ethylene-related hook development cues
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential positive regulator of apical hook maintenance in dark-grown seedlings
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced HLS1 expression contributes to hook opening in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4; HLS1 overexpression rescues the opened-hook phenotype of bdg1-4
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; HLS1::GUS reporter; genetic rescue with 35S::HLS1
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HLS1 expression is decreased in bdg1-4 and lacs2-4. 2. HLS1 promoter activity is reduced in cuticle mutants. 3. Overexpression of HLS1 rescues the opened apical hook phenotype of bdg1-4, showing that reduced HLS1 is causal for impaired hook development.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reduced in dark-grown bdg1-4 and lacs2-4 hook tissues
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Active in apical hook region of dark-grown WT; reduced in cuticle mutants
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation affects hook curvature and seedling emergence-related growth programs
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be used to alter hook development or seedling emergence traits, but not directly a nutrient-metabolism target
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not directly linked to a nutritional metabolite; included because it is a primary mechanistic regulator validated in the Results.
